test_id_1.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
test_id_1.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider manual guidance is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-12-01 is older and mainly points providers back to eMedNY manuals for pharmacy claim guidance, while earlier policy text says dual eligible members are billed through Medicare rather than Medicaid for immunization services. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy chunk does not define dual eligible pharmacy claim billing in detail; it says, \"Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for additional program policy and billing guidance\" [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]. Earlier policy text states, \"Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], and also that \"Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023].", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance says, \"the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient\" and \"If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]. It also states that \"all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025], and that NYS Medicaid requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid so dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from enrolled providers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — Oct 01, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources treat Medicare as central to dual eligible billing: policy says dual eligible members \"will continue to access immunization services through Medicare\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], and the provider manual says pharmacy providers must \"submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].", "Both sources indicate Medicaid does not pay first for dual eligible claims: policy says \"Medicaid is the payer of last resort\" and pays only after other reimbursement sources are exhausted [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023], while the provider manual caps combined Medicare/Medicaid payment at the Medicaid-only amount and says no additional payment is made if Medicare exceeds the Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["Policy is less specific and mostly directs providers to the manuals for pharmacy claim guidance: \"Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for additional program policy and billing guidance\" [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]. The provider manual gives the operative billing rule for dual eligible pharmacy claims, including the payment cap and Medicare assignment requirement [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].", "Earlier policy language says, \"Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021], which is narrower and framed around immunization services, whereas the provider manual addresses dual eligible pharmacy claims generally and requires Medicare enrollment/claim submission for Medicare-covered services [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "caveats": "The newest policy chunk available here does not itself define dual eligible pharmacy claim billing; it points to the provider manual for guidance [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]. The provider manual is the more recent controlling source for this issue based on the provided dates [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025]."}

test_id_2.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
test_id_2.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for dual eligible pharmacy claims and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy source dated 2024-12-01 is older and mainly points providers back to eMedNY manuals and billing guidance rather than stating the detailed dual-eligible pharmacy claim rule itself. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22,23 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy evidence does not define a detailed dual-eligible pharmacy claim rule; it says practitioners should refer to the eMedNY provider manual for additional program policy and billing guidance: \"Practitioners should refer to the associated eMedNY provider manual web pages provided above, for additional program policy and billing guidance.\" [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024] Older policy guidance states that \"Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted\" and that \"potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid.\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023] It also states that \"Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare.\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance states that \"The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient\" and that if Medicare pays more than the Medicaid fee, \"no additional payment will be made\"; it also says \"The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025] For dual eligible members, \"all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members\" and pharmacies must have the needed Medicare enrollment when required for items billed to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22,23 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources treat Medicare as primary for dual eligible pharmacy claims and require Medicare-related billing before Medicaid payment is considered: \"potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid\" and Medicaid is \"the payer of last resort\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023]; the provider manual says pharmacies must \"submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025].", "Both sources limit Medicaid payment in dual-eligible situations rather than allowing unrestricted duplicate payment: policy says Medicaid pays only after other reimbursement is exhausted [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — Feb 01, 2023], and the provider manual says combined Medicare/Medicaid payment \"will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient\" and must be \"accepted as payment in full\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — Oct 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The policy excerpts are less detailed and, in the newest policy source, defer providers to the eMedNY manuals for \"additional program policy and billing guidance,\" whereas the provider manual gives the operative payment and claim rules directly. [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22,23 — Oct 01, 2025]", "Older policy language specifically says \"Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members\" and that dual eligible members \"will continue to access immunization services through Medicare,\" but the provider manual excerpt provided here does not repeat that immunization-specific limitation; instead, it focuses on Medicare enrollment, claim submission, and payment limits for Medicare-covered services. [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22,23 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "caveats": "The newest policy excerpt is a referral statement rather than a full dual-eligible pharmacy claim rule, so the provider manual should guide current operational billing questions. The policy excerpts include older background guidance that remains relevant, but the 2025 provider manual is the more current source for this issue. [New York State Medicaid Update December 2024 Volume 40 Number 13:4 — Dec 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22,23 — Oct 01, 2025]"}

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21,22 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources recognize that dual eligible pharmacy claims involve Medicare as part of the billing/payment framework rather than Medicaid paying as if the member were Medicaid-only. [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — Aug 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18,21,22 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "differences": ["The policy excerpt is narrower and says pharmacies “will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members,” with dual eligible members accessing immunization services through Medicare, while the provider manual addresses broader dual-eligible pharmacy claim payment and Medicare enrollment/assignment requirements. test_id_7.query: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_7.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims
test_id_7.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, provider guidance is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources say Medicaid is payer of last resort and that other liable coverage must be billed first, but the provider manual gives the clearest current rule that a responsible third party must pay first for primary payor responsibility. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid and that \"NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort\" because \"all other available resources [must] be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] Earlier policy language also states that \"Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts\" for certain drugs excluded from TPL coverage, including Medicare. [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — Nov 01, 2021]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance says that \"all available resources [must] be used before Medicaid considers payment,\" that a provider must \"pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid,\" and that \"If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] The billing guidelines also require the provider to determine whether the patient has other insurance and whether that insurance covers the service being billed. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18-19 — Jul 23, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources require other liable coverage to be billed before Medicaid: \"providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid\" and \"NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort.\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]", "Both sources place the primary-payment responsibility on the other payer when one exists: the policy says Medicaid pays only after other resources are used, and the manual says \"that responsible third party must pay first.\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "differences": ["The provider manual is more explicit about primary payor responsibility, stating that \"If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first,\" while the policy excerpt uses broader payer-of-last-resort language. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025]", "The policy excerpt includes a pharmacy-specific payment rule for certain excluded drugs: \"Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare,\" which is not stated in the provider manual excerpt. [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:16 — Nov 01, 2021] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "caveats": "Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for the policy source beyond the payer-of-last-resort and excluded-drug payment language quoted above; the provider manual excerpt is the more current and more specific guidance for primary payer responsibility. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7-8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]"}

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid providers must bill liable third parties before Medicaid and that Medicaid is the payer of last resort: \"NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. test_id_21.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_21.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare
test_id_21.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, the provider manual is more up to date overall for this issue and should receive more weight by default. Both sources require billing other liable coverage before Medicaid, but the provider manual specifically says Medicare must be billed first when services are covered by both Medicare and Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy source says NYS Medicaid providers must bill applicable third parties that may be liable before billing Medicaid, because “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort” and “all other available resources” must be used before Medicaid considers payment. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] Earlier policy updates say the same general rule, including that providers must bill “applicable third parties” before billing NYS Medicaid and that Medicaid is the “payor of last resort.” [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5:4 — May 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:2, 3 — Apr 01, 2022]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual source states that “Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid” and that “[s]ervices covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare.” It also says pharmacies enrolled as billing providers are required to demonstrate participation in Medicare. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also states that a provider must pursue available other coverage before submitting a claim to Medicaid and that if a responsible third party is the primary payor, “that responsible third party must pay first.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources require other liable coverage to be billed before Medicaid: policy says providers are “required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid,” and the manual says available other coverage must be pursued before submitting a claim to Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]", "Both sources treat Medicaid as secondary to other available payment sources: policy says “NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort,” and the manual says “Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "differences": ["The provider manual is more specific for Medicare: it says “Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare,” while the policy source only states the broader rule to bill “applicable third parties” before Medicaid and does not single out Medicare in the cited April 2025 pharmacy reminder. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]", "The policy source frames the rule generally as Medicaid being the “payer of last resort” for all liable third parties, while the provider manual adds operational Medicare enrollment language and a Medicare-first billing instruction for dual-covered services. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "caveats": "The provided policy excerpts do not contain a pharmacy-specific Medicare-first instruction as explicit as the provider manual; they state only the general third-party-liability rule. The provider manual is newer and should guide current pharmacy billing practice for this issue unless you are reviewing a historical policy period. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — Apr 01, 2025]"}

[New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15:9 — Oct 01, 2020]", "provider_manual_definition": "The provider manual states that federal regulations require all available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment, and that Medicaid regulations at 18 NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — Jul 01, 2020] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — Oct 01, 2025]"], "differences": ["The provider manual is newer and adds an explicit rule that failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsible third party’s obligation to pay first; the policy excerpts do not state that specific exception. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:6 — Jul 01, 2022]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy evidence says NYS Medicaid FFS covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported in the listed compendia for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service: “NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act §1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — Jul 01, 2022] Earlier policy also states billing responsibility is on the pharmacy: “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond practitioner-administered drug coverage and pharmacy billing responsibility.", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual guidance says: “The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] It also states that NYS Medicaid recognizes home infusion drugs as drugs requiring practitioner administration to be available through both benefits: “NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025].", "Both sources indicate billing/coverage depends on the correct benefit or payer pathway rather than a single universal payment method: “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — Jul 01, 2020] and “Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - The manual says home infusion drugs and supplies are not covered under a bundled payment and must be billed as either a pharmacy or medical benefit, while the newest policy source dated 2022-07-01 addresses coverage for practitioner-administered drugs but does not specifically define home infusion billing responsibility.", "policy_definition": "The newest policy evidence says NYS Medicaid FFS covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported by the listed compendia for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service: “NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act §1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01] Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define home infusion drug coverage or billing responsibility for the policy source.", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual evidence states: “The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01] It also says NYS Medicaid recognizes home infusion drugs as drugs requiring practitioner administration to be available “by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]", "similarities": ["Both sources address drugs administered by a practitioner route and tie coverage to Medicaid-covered use rather than a blanket home-infusion-specific payment rule: “drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in the practitioner’s office are covered...” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01] and “NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider...” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources place billing/coverage within Medicaid benefit structure rather than a separate home-infusion bundle: the policy source covers certain administered drugs under NYS Medicaid FFS [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01], and the manual says home infusion drugs and supplies “must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual is explicit that there is no bundled payment for home infusion and that home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as pharmacy or medical benefit: “does not provide a bundled payment... must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.” [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01] The policy source does not provide that home-infusion-specific billing rule.", "The policy source’s billing responsibility language is pharmacy-focused and general: “It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.” [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01] The provider manual excerpt provided does not assign billing responsibility to the pharmacy in those terms, instead stating the drugs/supplies must be billed as pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]."], "caveats": "The provided policy excerpts do not contain a home-infusion-specific coverage rule, so the policy-side answer is incomplete for this exact concept. The provider manual is the more recent and more directly applicable source for home infusion coverage and billing, but the excerpts do not identify a single responsible billing entity beyond requiring billing through the pharmacy or medical benefit."}

test_id_55.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated 2025-10-01, home infusion drugs are not covered through a bundled payment; they must be billed as either a pharmacy benefit or a medical benefit, so the provider manual should guide current billing/coverage questions by default. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — May 01, 2023]", "policy_definition": "The newest policy evidence says NYRx covers covered products and services when the prescriber is enrolled: \"NYRx will pay for covered products and services for NYS Medicaid members when their prescriber is enrolled in NYRx.\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — May 01, 2023] Older policy guidance also states that certain practitioner-administered drugs are available through both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit and \"may be billed directly to the Me...\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6-7 — Aug 01, 2019]. Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents beyond those general practitioner-administered-drug billing rules. [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9:5 — May 01, 2023]", "provider_manual_definition": "The newest provider manual source states: \"Home Infusion The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] It further says, \"Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit.\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] The manual also says home infusion drugs are among drugs requiring administration by a health care provider that are available through both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources recognize that drugs requiring administration by a practitioner/provider can be available through both the medical/pharmacy benefit structure: \"available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — Oct 01, 2025] and \"available to members by way of both the Medical Benefit and Pharmacy Benefit\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6-7 — Aug 01, 2019].", "Both sources tie payment to billing through the appropriate Medicaid pathway rather than a single bundled home-infusion payment: \"Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — Oct 01, 2025] and \"may be billed directly to the Me...\" for practitioner-administered drugs [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6-7 — Aug 01, 2019]."], "differences": ["The provider manual is explicit that there is no bundled payment for home infusion: \"does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - The newest provider manual excerpt only states that “The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology,” but it is in the context of billing both a drug and administration and does not mention naloxone specifically. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]", "similarities": ["Both sources address reimbursement/payment through pharmacy billing rules rather than a separate naloxone-specific payment rule: the policy says naloxone OTC must be dispensed under specified conditions, including standing order compliance, and the manual says the medication is reimbursed when billed via NDC using reimbursement methodology. [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4-5 — Feb 01, 2024] [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]", "Both sources tie reimbursement to compliance with applicable billing/claim requirements: the policy requires dispensing “according to: Food and Drug Administration (FDA) guidelines; NYS laws, rules, and regulations,” and the manual says claims must be submitted separately and billed using the ordering prescriber’s NPI and NDC for the drug. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025]", "The provider manual excerpt is broader and billing-focused, stating generally that “The pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology,” while the policy excerpt is narrower and specifically addresses OTC naloxone under Medicaid coverage rules. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — Oct 01, 2025] [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:4 — Feb 01, 2024]"], "caveats": "The provider manual excerpts provided do not contain a direct naloxone reimbursement definition, so current naloxone-specific guidance is grounded primarily in the 2024 Medicaid Update policy excerpt.
